title: Virtue Ethics & the Ethics of Care
simple_html:
Most ethical theories ask "What should I do?" Virtue ethics asks a different question: "What kind of person should I be?" It focuses on character — developing good traits like honesty, courage, fairness, and integrity — rather than on rules or outcomes.

The founder, Aristotle, taught that a virtue is the "golden mean" between two extremes. Courage, for example, sits between cowardice (too little) and recklessness (too much). We become virtuous by practice and habit, like learning a skill.

The ethics of care

A related approach, the ethics of care, says morality is rooted in relationships and responsibility for others, not abstract rules. It values empathy, attentiveness, and meeting the real needs of people we are connected to.

Why it matters

These theories explain why company culture and character matter. Good rules fail if people lack good character. Hiring for integrity and building a caring workplace can do more than any rulebook.

Key takeaways

  • Virtue ethics focuses on character, not just actions.
  • A virtue is the golden mean between excess and deficiency.
  • Character is built through habit and practice.
  • The ethics of care centers relationships, empathy, and meeting needs.
  • Both shift attention from rules to who we are and how we relate.

Ask not only "is this allowed?" but "would a person of good character do this?"
intermediate_html:
Virtue ethics and the ethics of care are agent-centered rather than act-centered theories. Instead of asking which action is right (deontology) or which produces the best outcome (utilitarianism), they ask what kind of person — and what kind of relationships — we should cultivate.

Aristotelian virtue ethics

Aristotle’s Nicomachean Ethics argues that the goal of human life is eudaimonia — flourishing or living well — achieved by exercising virtue. A virtue (arete, excellence of character) is a stable disposition to feel and act well. Aristotle’s central tool is the doctrine of the mean: each virtue lies between two vices, an excess and a deficiency.

Deficiency (vice)Virtue (mean)Excess (vice)
CowardiceCourageRecklessness
StinginessGenerosityWastefulness
Self-deprecationHonesty/truthfulnessBoastfulness

Crucially, the mean is relative to the situation, not a mathematical midpoint, and it is identified by phronesis (practical wisdom) — the experienced judgment to know the right thing, at the right time, in the right amount. Virtues are acquired through habituation: we become honest by acting honestly until it becomes second nature.

The ethics of care

Developed by Carol Gilligan (1982) and Nel Noddings (1984), care ethics arose partly as a critique of male-centered "justice" reasoning. Gilligan found that women often reason morally through a "different voice" — emphasizing relationships, responsibility, and context rather than abstract rights and rules. Care ethics holds that morality is grounded in caring relationships and the concrete needs of particular others. Its key values are attentiveness (noticing needs), responsibility, competence in caregiving, and responsiveness of the cared-for (Tronto, 1993). It rejects the assumption that the impartial, detached stance of utilitarianism and deontology is morally superior.

Worked example: the layoff

A manager must cut staff. A utilitarian minimizes total harm by the numbers; a deontologist ensures fair, transparent process and honors contractual rights. A virtue ethicist asks how a person of integrity, compassion, and courage would conduct the layoff: honestly, without scapegoating, taking personal responsibility. A care ethicist attends to the concrete needs of affected employees — generous notice, references, outplacement help, preserving dignity and ongoing relationships. The frameworks are complementary: virtue and care humanize the decision that the other theories analyze.

How to apply it

  1. Identify the relevant virtues for the role (honesty, courage, fairness, prudence).
  2. Ask the exemplar question: what would a person of excellent character do here?
  3. Aim for the mean — neither deficiency nor excess, calibrated to context.
  4. Map the relationships and needs at stake (care lens) — who depends on you, and what do they actually need?
  5. Build habits and culture — design hiring, mentoring, and norms that cultivate virtue, since character is habituated.

Strengths and limits

These approaches capture the moral importance of character, motivation, relationships, and context that rule-based theories miss, and they map well onto leadership and organizational culture. Their limits: they offer less concrete action-guidance (what exactly should I do?), virtues can conflict (honesty vs. kindness), "the virtuous person" can be hard to identify, and care ethics risks partiality and caregiver burnout if it neglects justice toward distant others. In practice they complement, rather than replace, utilitarian and deontological reasoning.
full_html:
Virtue ethics and the ethics of care are the two leading agent-centered and relationship-centered alternatives to the act-centered theories of utilitarianism and deontology. Rather than asking "which act is right?" they ask "what kind of person should I become?" (virtue ethics) and "how should I respond to those who depend on me?" (care ethics). Both have surged in influence as scholars recognized that rules and calculations alone cannot explain moral excellence, leadership, or the texture of real relationships.

Aristotelian virtue ethics

The locus classicus is Aristotle’s Nicomachean Ethics (trans. 2009). Aristotle begins from teleology: everything has a function (ergon), and the human function is rational activity in accordance with virtue. The end of human life is eudaimonia — best translated as flourishing or living well, not mere pleasure. Eudaimonia is achieved through the exercise of virtue (arete), a settled disposition of character. Aristotle divides virtues into moral virtues (courage, temperance, generosity, justice — acquired by habituation) and intellectual virtues (notably phronesis or practical wisdom — acquired by teaching and experience). His signature doctrine of the mean holds that each moral virtue is a mean between two vices, one of excess and one of deficiency: courage between cowardice and recklessness, generosity between stinginess and prodigality, proper pride between humility and vanity. The mean is not arithmetic but relative to the agent and circumstances, discerned by practical wisdom. Critically, virtue is a matter of habituation (hexis): "we become just by doing just acts, temperate by doing temperate acts" — character is built, like a craft, through repeated practice.

The modern revival

Virtue ethics lay dormant under the dominance of Kantian and utilitarian theory until G. E. M. Anscombe’s landmark essay "Modern Moral Philosophy" (1958) argued that rule-based ethics had become incoherent without a divine lawgiver and urged a return to character and the virtues. Alasdair MacIntyre’s After Virtue (1981) reconstructed virtue ethics around practices, internal goods, and traditions, arguing that modern moral discourse is in disarray. Rosalind Hursthouse (1999) developed a virtue-based account of right action: "an action is right iff it is what a virtuous agent would characteristically do in the circumstances." Robert Solomon (1992) applied Aristotelian virtue directly to business in Ethics and Excellence, treating the corporation as a community within which individuals pursue excellence and the virtues of integrity, fairness, and honesty.

The ethics of care

Care ethics emerged from feminist moral psychology. Carol Gilligan’s In a Different Voice (1982) challenged Lawrence Kohlberg’s stage theory of moral development, which ranked abstract "justice" reasoning highest and tended to score women lower. Gilligan argued this reflected a male bias: many people, disproportionately women, reason in a "different voice" oriented to care, relationships, and responsibility rather than to impersonal rights and rules — not an inferior mode but a complementary one. Nel Noddings’s Caring (1984) developed care as the foundation of ethics, centered on the relation between the one-caring and the cared-for, emphasizing engrossment in the other’s reality and motivational displacement toward their needs. Joan Tronto (1993) articulated four phases and corresponding virtues of care: attentiveness (caring about — noticing need), responsibility (taking care of), competence (caregiving), and responsiveness (care-receiving). Care ethics is distinctive in rejecting impartiality and abstraction as the hallmarks of moral maturity; it holds that our concrete attachments and particular responsibilities are morally fundamental, not distractions to be transcended.

Worked example: closing a long-serving plant

A firm decides to close a 40-year-old plant in a small town. Utilitarianism tallies aggregate welfare; deontology checks that contractual and legal rights and fair process are honored. The virtue lens asks how leaders of genuine character would act: with courage (delivering the news personally rather than by memo), honesty (no euphemism or scapegoating), justice (equitable severance), and practical wisdom (calibrating generosity to what the situation truly warrants — the mean between reckless over-promising and stingy minimalism). The care lens attends to the concrete web of relationships: long-tenured workers, their families, the dependent community. It asks what these particular people actually need — extended notice, retraining, outplacement, healthcare bridging, honest references — and treats the ongoing relationship and the workers’ dignity as morally central. The two agent-centered frameworks do not replace the calculations; they govern the manner and spirit in which a hard decision is executed, which is often where ethical failure actually occurs.

Nuances, debates, and common mistakes

  • The action-guidance objection: critics charge that "do what a virtuous person would do" is unhelpfully circular and gives less concrete direction than a rule or a calculation. Hursthouse’s reply is that mature moral life genuinely requires phronesis, not algorithms.
  • Conflicting virtues: honesty may conflict with kindness, courage with prudence; the mean and practical wisdom must adjudicate, but reasonable people disagree.
  • Cultural relativity of virtues: which traits count as virtues can vary across cultures and eras (a concern linking to relativism debates).
  • Partiality and justice: care ethics’ focus on near relationships risks neglecting distant strangers and can shade into favoritism; many theorists pair care with a justice constraint (held, 2006).
  • Caregiver exploitation: an unqualified ethic of care can entrench burdens on those (historically women) expected to do the caring — Tronto stresses care must be politically and justly distributed.
  • Common exam error: describing virtue ethics as just "being a good person" without the technical apparatus of eudaimonia, the mean, habituation, and phronesis; or equating care ethics with mere sentimentality rather than a structured relational theory.

Application to business and leadership

Virtue and care ethics map onto organizational reality better than abstract theories: they explain why culture, hiring for character, role-modeling, and mentoring shape conduct more than compliance manuals, and why ethical leadership (which the course treats later) is largely about exemplifying virtues. They reframe the firm as a moral community and a web of stakeholder relationships rather than a nexus of contracts.

Cross-links

This lesson complements the act-centered theories of Consequentialism & Utilitarianism (Lesson 2) and Deontology & Rights (Lesson 3), supplying the character and relationship dimensions they omit. Care ethics’ relational view anticipates CSR & Stakeholder Theory (Lesson 7). The role of habituation and culture feeds directly into Ethical Decision-Making (Lesson 6) and the ethical-leadership themes of Governance, Globalization & Contemporary Issues (Lesson 8).
